statement: Hydration only matters on workout days.
explanation: MYTH. Hydration is an every-single-day habit. Focus on fluids all day, not just around workouts — that's how you show up ready.
fun_fact: Carrying a water bottle everywhere is one of the simplest habits shared by top athletes.

statement: Losing even a little sweat weight can hurt your performance.
explanation: FACT. Losing just a small percentage of your body weight in sweat measurably slows you down. Staying topped up keeps your speed and endurance where they belong.
fun_fact: For a 200-pound athlete, that tipping point is only about 4 pounds of sweat.

statement: Waiting until you're thirsty is a solid hydration strategy.
explanation: MYTH. Thirst shows up late to the game. By the time you feel it, you're already behind. Hydrate early and often — before, during, and after exercise.
fun_fact: Hard exercise can actually blunt your thirst signal — another reason to drink on a schedule.

statement: You should rehydrate within a couple hours after exercise.
explanation: FACT. The clock starts when training ends. Replacing your fluid losses within about 2 hours sets up your recovery and tomorrow's performance.
fun_fact: Rehydration is the fastest piece of recovery you can control.

statement: You need a sports drink for every workout.
explanation: MYTH. For sessions under an hour, water does the job perfectly. Sports drinks earn their place during long, intense, sweaty training.
fun_fact: Electrolytes come in food too — you don't always need a bottle or packet.

statement: Electrolytes should be part of your whole day, not just your workout.
explanation: FACT. Water and electrolytes consumed throughout the day keep your fluid and electrolyte levels optimal — so you start every session ready to go.
fun_fact: A balanced diet covers most electrolyte needs without any special products.

statement: Sweat is just water leaving your body.
explanation: MYTH. Sweat carries sodium, chloride, potassium, magnesium, and calcium out with it. That's why hard-sweating athletes replace more than just water.
fun_fact: A runner sweating hard for an hour can lose up to a teaspoon of salt.

statement: Your meals are part of your training, just like reps in the gym.
explanation: FACT. Every plate drives your energy, hydration, and recovery. Fuel with purpose and every practice gets better.
fun_fact: Your 'performance plates' directly affect your energy levels, hydration status, and recovery potential.

statement: Training hard can make up for poor fueling.
explanation: MYTH. You can't out-train a bad diet. Without proper fueling, your full athletic potential stays locked away — no matter how many hours you grind.
fun_fact: Quality gym hours PLUS quality fueling is the formula dietitians give college athletes.

statement: Your plate should look the same in-season and offseason.
explanation: MYTH. Your needs change with your training. In-season and hard days call for more energy foods; offseason and light days shift toward more fruits, veggies, and protein.
fun_fact: In the offseason, grains may decrease and lean protein may increase to support body composition goals.

statement: Fruits and vegetables are your plate's antioxidant defense squad.
explanation: FACT. Berries, spinach, broccoli, carrots, and friends deliver antioxidants that power recovery and keep your immune system game-ready.
fun_fact: Colorful plates win — each color brings different protective nutrients.

statement: Skipping breakfast saves time and doesn't affect performance.
explanation: MYTH. Skipping breakfast leaves you tired and sluggish, then ravenous at night — which wrecks your sleep and tomorrow's appetite. Break the cycle with morning fuel.
fun_fact: Even a quick banana with peanut butter counts as breaking the fast like a champion.

statement: A big meal right before bed sets you up for a great morning.
explanation: MYTH. Late-night feasts make it harder to fall asleep and kill your morning appetite. Spread your fuel through the day and both your sleep and breakfast improve.
fun_fact: Consistent daytime eating naturally fixes the late-night hunger problem.

statement: A balanced meal combines lean protein, carbs, and fruits or vegetables.
explanation: FACT. That's the winning formula: turkey sub with pretzels and fruit, pasta with meat sauce and salad, or chicken stir-fry with rice and veggies. Balanced and built to perform.
fun_fact: Fruits and veggies bring micronutrients that round out every meal.

statement: Flexibility matters — some days won't go as planned, and that's okay.
explanation: FACT. Some days are busier than others. Stay flexible, focus on meeting your overall needs, and get back on your routine at the next meal. Progress beats perfection.
fun_fact: Sports dietitians build flexibility into every athlete's plan on purpose.

statement: One hour a week of shopping and food prep can upgrade your entire week of fueling.
explanation: FACT. Fail to plan, plan to fail. One planned hour of shopping and prep positively impacts every food choice you make all week — that's a competitive advantage.
fun_fact: How athletes manage their time and feed their body can make or break performance gains.

statement: Frozen vegetables are less nutritious than fresh ones.
explanation: MYTH. Frozen veggies are just as nutrient-packed as fresh, they're fast and easy, and they never go bad in the back of the fridge. Stock up proudly.
fun_fact: Frozen fruit is a smoothie game-changer — no chopping, no waste.

statement: Shopping while hungry is a great way to discover new foods.
explanation: MYTH. Hungry shopping = splurge shopping. Eat first, bring your list, and stick to it. Discipline at the store shows up on your plate.
fun_fact: Store brands match name-brand quality at a lower price — smart athletes save.

statement: If it's on sale, it belongs in your cart.
explanation: MYTH. A deal on food you don't need is still money and nutrition lost. Don't rationalize the sale — run your list like a game plan.
fun_fact: BOGO deals are only wins when they're on foods already in your plan.

statement: Convenience foods like pre-cut veggies and rotisserie chicken can be worth the extra cost.
explanation: FACT. Weigh the cost of convenience: if pre-cut veggies, hard-boiled eggs, or a rotisserie chicken get healthy food into you faster, that's money well spent.
fun_fact: Most frozen dinners are the exception — low in nutrients and overpriced. Frozen stir-fry kits are the better play.

statement: Before training you fuel for the work you EXPECT to do; after, you fuel for the work you DID do.
explanation: FACT. Pre-fuel matches the session ahead — easy day, balanced snack; hard day, carb-focused. Afterward, your recovery plate matches what you actually did. That's fueling with intention.
fun_fact: This expect/did framework comes straight from sports dietitians who fuel elite athletes.

statement: Training on empty makes you tougher and fitter.
explanation: MYTH. Skipping fuel before high-intensity training blunts your aerobic gains over time and risks low energy availability. Always pre-fuel your hard sessions — that's how you actually get faster.
fun_fact: Chronic under-fueling has a name — RED-S — and it can sideline even dedicated athletes.

statement: WHEN you eat matters, not just WHAT you eat.
explanation: FACT. Within-day energy balance — matching your intake to your training through the day — is a key performance factor. Same food, better timing, better results.
fun_fact: Your body switches between carbs and fat for fuel depending on training intensity and what you've eaten.

statement: Rest days deserve fuel too.
explanation: FACT. Energy needs spread across your whole week — including rest days — to support recovery and keep your energy availability strong. Rest days are when you rebuild.
fun_fact: During heavy training weeks, dietitians plan fuel across the entire week, not just workout days.

statement: The goal of fueling during long training is keeping your blood sugar stable.
explanation: FACT. Steady blood glucose = steady energy = steady performance. That's why endurance athletes fuel by the hour during long sessions.
fun_fact: A sports drink during a 60-75 minute session is often all it takes to hold steady.
